ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for camarones con hongos (shrimp with mushrooms). FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific mixed shrimp-mushroom dish term is absent from the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Shrimps and prawns) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a sautéed shrimp-mushroom dish in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for camarones con hongos or a directly equivalent sautéed shrimp-mushroom dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of camarones con hongos is approximately 250 g, comprising sautéed shrimp and mushrooms plated as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, cooked (USDA SR Legacy #15152) and Mushrooms, white, cooked (USDA SR Legacy #11261) — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for camarones con hongos as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked white mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), combined in an approximate 70:30 shrimp-to-mushroom ratio by weight, with an allowance for cooking oil (approximately 5 g olive oil per 100 g dish). Energy and macros are calculated from these components. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; shrimp and seafood dishes analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for camarones con hongos. Shrimp is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other low-carbohydrate seafood dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 2.8 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 1.1, confirming very low GL. This is consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content of the dish.

hypertension.guidance: Camarones con hongos contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g, which can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake in a full restaurant serving (~800 mg per plate); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 15, camarones con hongos has a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the dish is not served with high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or tortillas.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shrimp is relatively high in dietary cholesterol (~142 mg per 100 g), and the dish carries moderate sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation, favour olive-oil preparation, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to offset cholesterol impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Camarones con Hongos is a Mexican seafood dish consisting of sautéed shrimp (camarones) and mushrooms (hongos), typically prepared with garlic, onion, chili, lime juice, cilantro, and butter or olive oil. The dish is a staple of Mexican coastal and interior cuisine, prized for its umami-rich, earthy flavour from the mushrooms combined with the sweet, briny taste of fresh shrimp. Texture is tender-firm from the shrimp and soft-meaty from the mushrooms. In Singapore, it is served at Mexican restaurants as a main course, priced around SGD 28. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (~4.5 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, which is a consideration for hypertension management. Shrimp provides significant selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is naturally gluten-free and can be made dairy-free by substituting butter with oil.
common_user_queries: Is camarones con hongos good for diabetics?
How many calories in shrimp with mushrooms?
What does camarones con hongos taste like?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, USDA) contains a direct entry for camarones con hongos as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked shrimp and cooked mushrooms in an approximate ratio, with an oil allowance. GI is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate seafood dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy (cooked shrimp FDC 175180 and cooked white mushrooms FDC 169251) in a 70:30 ratio with ~5 g olive oil per 100 g dish allowance. A direct composite dish entry does not exist in any approved primary source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are -1 as these specific fatty acid breakdowns are not consistently reported or easily estimable for the composite dish from the USDA SR Legacy component sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite dish; while shrimp contains trace vitamin D, a confirmed value for the composite dish is not available from the source. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible for olive-oil-sautéed shrimp and mushrooms per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate seafood dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 6. Halal status is assumed as shrimp is generally considered halal in mainstream Islamic jurisprudence, but restaurant-level halal certification was not confirmed from the field survey source. 7. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant main-course plate; actual restaurant portion may vary. 8. Sodium of 320 mg per 100 g is an estimate; restaurant preparation may use significantly more salt or seasoning.

improvement_actions_taken: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure by explicitly documenting the reason and source for all -1 values in per_100g (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_d_mcg) within the 'notes' field, as required by the Sentinel values documentation rule.
Constructed full nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked shrimp + cooked mushrooms + oil allowance) as no direct composite dish entry exists in approved sources.
Estimated GI of 15 by analogy with low-carbohydrate seafood dishes from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021).
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance: Mexican cuisine does not require non-Latin-script mandatory names; romanized_local name provided.
Set ontology match types to only allowed values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
